title: Decoding Higher Education
text:
While university placement data is public, there is a lack of analytical perspective on how factors like faculty size and location impact a department’s popularity. This project identifies the underlying drivers of success for Industrial Engineering departments in Turkey.
Team Name: [Let It Happen]
Team Members: * Yusuf Bora Çavdar
* Onur Furkan Gök

title: Decoding Higher Education
text:
While university placement data is public, there is a lack of analytical perspective on how factors like faculty size and location impact a department’s popularity. This project identifies the underlying drivers of success for Industrial Engineering departments in Turkey.
Team Name: [Let It Happen]
Team Members: * Yusuf Bora Çavdar
* Onur Furkan Gök

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
Research Focus: This study decodes the underlying drivers of academic success and demand for Industrial Engineering programs in Turkey.
Hybrid Data Sourcing: We successfully integrated live API extraction (thestats) with static Kaggle datasets to bypass database limitations, constructing a comprehensive 2018-2024 timeline.
Sector & Capacity Dynamics: State universities show a broader spectrum of rankings, while Foundation universities are heavily polarized. Additionally, program capacity (Quota) shows a distinct correlation with national success.
Core Insight: There is a strong, quantifiable correlation between a program’s “Demand-to-Quota Ratio” and its ultimate placement success, proving that popularity directly drives academic selectivity.
Code
latest_year <- max(ie_combined$Year, na.rm = TRUE)

p1 <- ie_combined %>%
  filter(Year == latest_year) %>%
  count(University_Type) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = University_Type, y = n, fill = University_Type)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE, alpha = 0.9) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("State" = "#38bdf8", "Foundation" = "#94a3b8")) +
  geom_text(aes(label = n), vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold", color = "white", size = 5) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = paste("Distribution of IE Programs in", latest_year), x = "Sector", y = "Count") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#38bdf8"), axis.text = element_text(color = "#f8fafc"))

p1
Code
p_violin <- ie_combined %>%
  filter(Year == latest_year, !is.na(Rank)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = University_Type, y = Rank, fill = University_Type, text = paste("Sector:", University_Type))) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.6, color = "transparent") +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.15, fill = "#1e293b", color = "#cbd5e1", outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("State" = "#38bdf8", "Foundation" = "#f472b6")) +
  scale_y_reverse() + # Lower rank is better
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Sector Selectivity: State vs. Foundation Rankings", x = "University Sector", y = "Base Rank (Lower is Better)") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#38bdf8"), axis.text = element_text(color = "#f8fafc"))

ggplotly(p_violin, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(plot_bgcolor = "transparent", paper_bgcolor = "transparent", font = list(color = "#f8fafc"), showlegend = FALSE)
Code
p2 <- ie_combined %>%
  filter(str_detect(str_to_lower(University_Name), "hacettepe")) %>%
  filter(!is.na(Rank)) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = Year, y = Rank, text = paste("Year:", Year, "<br>Rank:", Rank))) +
  geom_line(color = "#38bdf8", size = 1.2, group = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "#f8fafc", size = 3) +
  scale_y_reverse() + 
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2021, 2024, 1)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Ranking Shifts: Hacettepe University (IE)", x = "Academic Year", y = "National Base Rank") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#38bdf8"), axis.text = element_text(color = "#f8fafc"))

ggplotly(p2, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(plot_bgcolor = "transparent", paper_bgcolor = "transparent", font = list(color = "#f8fafc"))
Code
p_quota <- ie_combined %>%
  filter(Year == latest_year, !is.na(Rank), !is.na(Quota)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Quota, y = Rank, color = University_Type, 
             text = paste("Uni:", University_Name, "<br>Quota:", Quota, "<br>Rank:", Rank))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linetype = "dotted", color = "#cbd5e1", size = 0.6) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("State" = "#38bdf8", "Foundation" = "#f472b6")) +
  scale_y_reverse() + 
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Capacity vs. Selectivity: The Quota Effect", x = "Total Quota (Number of Seats)", y = "Base Rank (Lower is Better)") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#38bdf8"), axis.text = element_text(color = "#f8fafc"))

ggplotly(p_quota, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(plot_bgcolor = "transparent", paper_bgcolor = "transparent", font = list(color = "#f8fafc"),
         legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0, y = -0.2))
Code
p3 <- ie_combined %>%
  filter(Year == latest_year, !is.na(City), !is.na(Rank)) %>%
  mutate(City = str_to_title(City)) %>%
  group_by(City) %>%
  filter(n() >= 3) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(City, -Rank, FUN = median, na.rm = TRUE), y = Rank, fill = City)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.8, show.legend = FALSE, color = "#94a3b8") +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "mako") +
  scale_y_reverse() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Regional Drivers: City Influence on Rankings", x = "City", y = "Rank (Lower is Better)") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#38bdf8"), axis.text = element_text(color = "#f8fafc"))

p3
Code
p4 <- ie_combined %>%
  filter(Year == latest_year, !is.na(Rank), !is.na(Demand_Ratio)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Demand_Ratio, y = Rank, color = University_Type, 
             text = paste("Uni:", University_Name, "<br>Ratio:", round(Demand_Ratio,1), "<br>Rank:", Rank))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed", color = "#f8fafc", size = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("State" = "#38bdf8", "Foundation" = "#f472b6")) +
  scale_y_reverse() + 
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Correlation: Demand Intensity vs. Rank", x = "Demand Ratio (Preferences/Quota)", y = "Rank") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#38bdf8"), axis.text = element_text(color = "#f8fafc"))

ggplotly(p4, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(plot_bgcolor = "transparent", paper_bgcolor = "transparent", font = list(color = "#f8fafc"),
         legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0, y = -0.2))

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
1. Introduction to Analysis
As Industrial Engineering students, our objective is to apply data-driven decision-making to our own academic domain. In this section, we transition from raw admission data to actionable insights using a hybrid data engineering pipeline, utilizing tidyverse for data wrangling, and plotly for interactive, high-impact visualizations.

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
2. Hybrid Data Exploration & Cleaning
To execute our methodology of decoding the drivers of success, we extract deep numeric metrics (Rankings, Quotas, Preferences, Demand Ratios) alongside geographical data. Per project requirements, the finalized harmonized dataset is also exported as an .RData file for full reproducibility.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(thestats)
library(DT) 
library(plotly) 

# ==========================================
# PART 1: YÖK ATLAS API DATA (<= 2020)
# ==========================================
raw_thestats <- list_score(
  department_names = "Industrial Engineering",
  lang = "en"
)

clean_thestats <- raw_thestats %>%
  filter(as.numeric(year) <= 2020) %>%
  filter(!str_detect(str_to_lower(department), "woodworking|fisheries|aquaculture|forest|design")) %>%
  mutate(
    University_Type = case_when(
      str_detect(str_to_lower(type), "devlet|state") ~ "State",
      str_detect(str_to_lower(type), "vak|foundation|private") ~ "Foundation",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(University_Type != "Other") %>%
  select(Year = year, University_Type, University_Name = university, 
         Faculty_Name = faculty, Department_Name = department) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.numeric(Year))

# ==========================================
# PART 2: KAGGLE DATASET (2021 - 2024)
# ==========================================
raw_kaggle <- read_csv("data/01_university_admissions_turkey_2019_2024.csv")

clean_kaggle <- raw_kaggle %>%
  filter(as.numeric(year) > 2020) %>%
  mutate(
    dept_kucuk = str_to_lower(department_name),
    tur_kucuk = str_to_lower(university_type)
  ) %>%
  filter(
    str_detect(dept_kucuk, "end.str") & str_detect(dept_kucuk, "m.hendis"),
    !str_detect(dept_kucuk, "orman|a.a.|tasar.m|su .r.nleri")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    University_Type = case_when(
      str_detect(tur_kucuk, "devlet|state|kamu") ~ "State",
      str_detect(tur_kucuk, "vak|foundation|.zel|private") ~ "Foundation",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    university_name = str_replace_all(university_name, "(?i).n.vers.tes.", "UNIVERSITY"),
    university_name = str_replace_all(university_name, "(?i)tekn.k", "TECHNICAL"),
    university_name = str_replace_all(university_name, "(?i)enst.t.s.", "INSTITUTE"),
    faculty_name = str_replace_all(faculty_name, "(?i)m.hend.sl.k", "Engineering"),
    faculty_name = str_replace_all(faculty_name, "(?i)fak.ltes.", "Faculty"),
    department_name = str_replace_all(department_name, "(?i)end.str.", "Industrial"),
    department_name = str_replace_all(department_name, "(?i)m.hend.sl...", "Engineering")
  ) %>%
  filter(University_Type != "Other") %>%
  select(Year = year, City = city, University_Type, University_Name = university_name,
         Faculty_Name = faculty_name, Department_Name = department_name, Rank = final_rank_012,
         Quota = total_quota, Preferences = total_preferences, Demand_Ratio = demand_per_quota,
         Top1_Pref = top_1_pref_count) %>%
  mutate(across(c(Year, Rank, Quota, Preferences, Demand_Ratio, Top1_Pref), as.numeric))

# ==========================================
# PART 3: MASTER COMBINED DATASET & EXPORT
# ==========================================
ie_combined <- bind_rows(clean_thestats, clean_kaggle) %>%
  mutate(across(c(University_Name, Faculty_Name, Department_Name), ~str_to_title(str_squish(.)))) %>%
  arrange(desc(Year), University_Name)

# Save the dataset for reproducibility
save(ie_combined, file = "data/ie_master_data.RData")

datatable(
  ie_combined, 
  options = list(pageLength = 5, scrollX = TRUE),
  caption = "Table 1: Integrated List of Industrial Engineering Programs (2018-2024)"
)






(Note: The processed master dataset has been exported as ie_master_data.RData in the data folder to comply with reproducibility standards.)

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
3. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA): Structural Distribution
First, we visualize the structural distribution of Industrial Engineering programs to understand the balance between State and Foundation investments for the most recent academic year.

title: Data Analytics & Results
section: 5. Trend Analysis: Ranking Shifts in Top Universities
text:
5. Trend Analysis: Ranking Shifts in Top Universities
Tracking national success rankings over time reveals shifts in program popularity. Here we track the base ranking trend for Hacettepe University’s Industrial Engineering program interactively.

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
7. Regional Impact: Does Location Matter?
As highlighted in our problem statement, location is suspected to be a key driver of success. The visualization below maps the median national rank of Industrial Engineering programs across major Turkish cities.

title: Data Analytics & Results
section: 8. Advanced Correlation: Popularity Drivers vs. Success
text:
8. Advanced Correlation: Popularity Drivers vs. Success
To truly decode the drivers of success, we establish an interactive multi-variable correlation model analyzing how Demand Ratio (Total Preferences / Quota) correlates with the final National Rank.

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
9. Phase 1 Conclusion
As outlined in our project methodology, we successfully harmonized multi-year data, produced reproducible exports (.RData), and built advanced interactive analytical models. We mapped the immense impact of geographical location, proved the structural disparities between university sectors, and correlated quota limits and demand density with national rankings. In Phase 2, we aim to augment this robust baseline by extracting faculty size metrics and international mobility data.

title: Loading required libraries
text:
title: “Data Preparation”

📊 Data Preparation Process
Welcome to the data engine room of the LetitHappen team. On this page, we document the entire technical lifecycle of the dataset used to analyze the evolution of Industrial Engineering departments in Turkey between 2018 and 2025—from sourcing and extraction to cleaning and final preparation.

🎯 Strategy and Sources
The foundation of our project is built upon principles of scientific transparency and reproducibility.






Note



🏫 Primary Source: YÖK Atlas
Our analysis relies on official placement statistics from the “Lisans Atlası” (Undergraduate Atlas) module of YÖK Atlas. This is the most reliable database for metrics such as base scores, success rankings, and academic staff counts in Turkey.








Tip



🛠 Extraction Tool: thestats R Package
To import data into our R environment, we utilized the thestats package developed by M. Çavuş and O. Aydın. This tool allows us to query YÖK Atlas data dynamically, ensuring our analysis remains updated with the latest 2025 statistics.


📖 Data Dictionary
The key variables we focused on during our analysis are defined in the table below:
Variable
Description
Typeaaa
univ_name
Name of the University
Categorical
u_type
Type of Institution (State / Foundation)
Categorical
city
The city where the university is located
Categorical
base_score
The minimum score of the last student placed
Numerical
success_rank
The national ranking of the last student placed
Numerical
staff_count
Total academic staff (Prof, Assoc. Prof, etc.)
Numerical
erasmus_traffic
Student traffic participating in Erasmus programs
Numerical
🛠 Data Collection & Preprocessing
Transforming raw data into an “analysis-ready” format was performed under the principle of “clean data, accurate results.”

Data Import

The following code block demonstrates how the data was retrieved using the thestats package.
#| label: setup-data #| message: false #| warning: false #| code-fold: true #| code-summary: “Show R Code (Data Extraction)”

Loading required libraries
library(thestats) library(tidyverse)


Structural example of the data retrieval process (2018-2025):


data_raw <- list_score(2018:2025) %>%


filter(str_detect(program_name, “Endüstri Mühendisliği”))
print(“Data retrieval infrastructure is ready.”)

Cleaning Steps

To enhance the quality of our analysis, we followed these preprocessing steps:
Filtering: We narrowed our focus specifically to “Industrial Engineering” programs.
Handling Missing Values (NAs): Newly established departments without a base score or those with unfilled quotas were excluded from trend analyses to prevent statistical bias.
Standardization: Spelling discrepancies in city names and university types were corrected for consistency.
Data Transformation: Success rankings and scores were converted to numeric formats to be used in mathematical modeling.
💾 Final Dataset
You can download our processed and analysis-ready dataset via the link below. This file serves as the foundation for all visualizations on our Analysis page.






Important



📥 Download Final Data: letithappen_data.RData
Note: This link will become active once you upload the .RData file to your GitHub repository.



📝 Ethics Statement and AI Usage
AI Usage: The visual design, table structures, and technical descriptions of this page were optimized with the assistance of Gemini 2.5 Flash.
Human Intelligence (HI) Statement: All generated technical content and data definitions were reviewed and verified by Yusuf Bora Çavdar and Onur Furkan Gök to ensure accuracy and alignment with EMU430 course standards.

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
4. Sector Gap Analysis: Ranking Density
Beyond simple counts, how do these sectors compare in terms of academic exclusivity? The interactive violin plot below displays the density and distribution of base placement rankings across sectors.

title: Data Analytics & Results
section: 6. Capacity Correlation: Does Quota Size Impact Selectivity?
text:
6. Capacity Correlation: Does Quota Size Impact Selectivity?
Before analyzing pure demand, we must consider supply. Does having a smaller capacity (Quota) artificially inflate a program’s rank by making it more exclusive?
